planting.note: Grown from divisions; set out potted plants in spring.

directions: Give this tall selection full sun and moderate moisture with good drainage. Its upright stems make a strong vertical accent in the border; shear back after flowering to renew. Long spikes of rich violet-blue flowers rise from early summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this vigorous catmint in full sun with average, well-drained soil and room for the clump to fill in. It flowers heavily and reblooms readily after a hard shear. Deep blue flowers set in dark violet calyces appear from early summer onward.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after cold-moist chilling, or set out divisions in spring.

directions: Chill the seed moist for a few weeks before sowing, then keep cool until it germinates. This tall, rhizomatous species can wander, so give it room or contain the roots. Large violet-blue flowers on upright stems bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set divisions of this large-flowered selection in full sun with room to spread, since it colonizes by runners. Cut it back after the first flush to keep it upright and encourage rebloom. Big lavender-blue flowers open on tall stems through summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Sow seed indoors on the surface and grow on before planting out. Unlike drought-loving catmints, this species prefers moist, humus-rich soil and tolerates part shade. Upright stems carry showy clusters of deep blue flowers in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this selection in full sun to part shade in moist, well-drained soil richer than most catmints prefer. Keep it watered in dry spells and cut back after flowering for a possible rebloom. Soft rose-pink flowers with darker calyces rise on tall stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors or plant divisions in spring; prefers cool summers.

directions: Sow seed indoors and grow on for planting in a cool, part-shaded spot with reliably moist soil. This unusual Himalayan catmint dislikes heat and drought, so mulch to keep roots cool. Airy sprays of pale, soft-yellow flowers dangle on branching stems through summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after a cold-moist period, or divide in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed, then surface-sow and keep cool until it sprouts. This tall, wiry species suits the back of a sunny border and draws many pollinators. Slender branching stems carry small pale lilac-blue flowers in airy spikes through summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions or cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this compact, richly colored catmint in full sun with lean, well-drained soil. Its dense habit resists flopping; shear after the first bloom to keep it fresh and reblooming. Deep blue flower spikes cover the gray-green foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set this early-blooming catmint in full sun and well-drained soil for a jump on the season. Shear it after the first flush to keep the mound compact and coax repeat flowering. Violet-blue flowers open noticeably ahead of most catmints in late spring.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this large-flowered catmint in full sun with average, well-drained soil. Its sturdy, mounded habit holds up without staking; trim after bloom to refresh. Big deep blue flowers with a green calyx give a bold display from early summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Give this large, upright catmint full sun and room to fill in, in lean well-drained soil. It keeps a strong vase shape rather than flopping open; shear after the first bloom for repeat color. Tall spikes of violet-blue flowers rise above gray-green foliage all summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this white-flowered catmint in full sun with sharp drainage as a low edger or groundcover. Its trailing habit knits together quickly; shear after bloom to keep it neat. Pure white flowers cover the gray-green mat in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter; needs a warm, dry summer spot.

directions: Sow seed indoors and grow on before planting in a hot, sharply drained site that stays dry in summer. This Mediterranean species forms tubers and resents wet winter soil, so give it grit or a raised bed. Dense upright spikes of purple flowers with dark bracts rise in early summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this large catmint in full sun with lean, well-drained soil and plenty of elbow room. It makes a billowing mass of bloom; shear it back after the first flush to renew the mound. Long spikes of light blue flowers cover the aromatic foliage from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or divide established clumps in early spring or fall.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and keep it damp, as light aids germination. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil; lift and divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Bright day-blooming yellow flowers cover the plant through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in spring or plant divisions into warm, workable soil.

directions: Sow onto the surface and keep evenly moist until seedlings establish. Reddish stems and blue-green foliage set off the golden cups, and the foliage often flushes bronze in cool weather. Expect clusters of yellow blooms through the first half of summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring; this cultivar is propagated vegetatively, not from seed.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing crowns so air moves freely. Red buds open from purple-tinged foliage into vivid yellow flowers, giving the plant its firework name. Divide every three or four years in early spring to renew flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; raise this selection from division rather than seed.

directions: Give it full sun and lean, free-draining soil for the best color. Foliage turns deep red in autumn while summer brings a long run of golden day-blooms. Lift and split the clump every few years to keep it tidy and free-flowering.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in warm, workable soil.

directions: Plant in full sun and average, well-drained ground. This tidy old selection produces neat clusters of bright yellow flowers over glossy green leaves through summer. Divide crowded clumps in early spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring.

directions: Site in full sun with good drainage for compact, upright growth. Pink-tinged buds open to clear yellow flowers held above dark, disease-resistant foliage. Shear lightly after the main flush and divide every few years to renew.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost danger has passed; propagate by division or cuttings.

directions: Plant in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping it on the dry side once established. This long-blooming selection carries large satiny yellow cups over a low spreading mat for months. Trim spent stems to encourage repeat flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; this species spreads readily by runners once settled.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press it in, as light helps germination. Give it a lean, sunny spot with room to roam, since it colonizes by underground runners and can become aggressive in rich soil. Cup-shaped pink flowers with paler centers open by day from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring.

directions: Set in full sun and well-drained soil where its spreading habit is welcome. This compact selection carries a heavy show of large, deep-pink flowers over a long season. Confine the roots or plant where it can wander, and cut back after bloom to tidy the mat.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Give it full sun and lean, quick-draining soil to curb excessive spread. Buds blush pink before opening to soft white cups that fade rose as they age. Shear the planting after the main flush to encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; flowers in the second year from a first-year rosette.

directions: Press seed onto open soil and keep it lightly moist until it germinates. This robust biennial builds a rosette the first year, then sends up a tall red-tinged stem lined with large golden flowers. Blooms open in the evening through summer and the plant seeds itself where happy.

planting.note: Direct sow into sandy, well-drained soil in spring.

directions: Sow shallowly in sandy or gritty soil and water sparingly, as this is a dryland plant. White flowers open in the evening and fade to pink by the next morning, carrying a sweet fragrance. It thrives in hot, lean sites and spreads modestly by rhizomes.

planting.note: Sow in spring in gritty, fast-draining soil; a cold spell can improve germination.

directions: Sow into sharp, gravelly soil and keep moisture low once seedlings appear. This stemless, tufted plant produces large white cups that sit almost on the foliage and blush pink as they age. Give it excellent drainage and full sun, and enjoy the sweetly scented evening flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; tolerates a range of soils including damp meadows.

directions: Surface sow and keep the ground moist until germination. This slender native holds small yellow day-flowers atop wiry stems and suits meadow or naturalized plantings. It blooms through early and midsummer and self-sows gently.

planting.note: Sow in spring; often grown as a short-lived perennial or biennial.

directions: Sow onto light, well-drained soil and keep it just moist. Slender stems carry fragrant yellow flowers that open in the evening and often flush pink or red as they fade. It appreciates a warm, sunny position and seeds itself around.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a warm, sunny, free-draining spot.

directions: Sow onto light soil and thin seedlings to give slim stems support from neighbors. Soft primrose-yellow flowers open in the evening, aging to apricot and pink tones for a two-toned effect. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and let it self-sow to persist.

planting.note: Sow into warm sandy soil after the last frost; a true desert annual.

directions: Sow shallowly in sandy, fast-draining soil and water only lightly. White flowers open in the evening and fade pink, and after seeding the dried stems curl into the papery cage that gives the plant its name. It needs heat, sun, and very little water to thrive.

planting.note: Sow after frost; often grown as an annual in cold-winter regions.

directions: Surface sow and keep lightly moist until seedlings appear. This tender species carries pink to white cup-shaped flowers over a long summer season. Give it warmth and full sun, and treat it as an annual where winters are hard.

planting.note: Sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot; can spread readily.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it damp until it sprouts. Small rose-pink day-flowers appear over a long season above spreading stems. Site in full sun and give it room, as it self-sows and travels where content.

planting.note: Sow spring or fall; forms a rosette the first year, flowers the next.

directions: Surface sow and keep the soil moist through establishment. This tall western biennial forms a large rosette before sending up a stout flowering stalk lined with big yellow evening flowers. It favors moist, sunny ground and reseeds to keep a colony going.

planting.note: Sow in spring in lean, sharply drained soil.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it lightly moist until germination. Silvery, felted foliage contrasts with huge lemon-yellow cups on this trailing selection. Give it hot sun and gritty drainage, avoiding rich or wet soil that dulls the silver leaves.

planting.note: Sow in spring; a selection prized for drought tolerance and fine foliage.

directions: Sow shallowly into lean, well-drained soil and water sparingly once established. Narrow, silver-shimmered leaves set off large soft-yellow flowers over a long summer season. It excels in hot, dry sites and rock gardens where drainage is excellent.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring.

directions: Set into hot, sunny, sharply drained ground and keep water low. Red stems and buds give a smoldering contrast to the large yellow cups this selection is known for. Trim back spent growth to keep the sprawling mat tidy.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Give this compact selection full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil. Its neat, low habit carries large golden cups making it well suited to rock gardens and troughs. Keep it lean and dry to prevent flopping and leggy growth.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost, or grow as a short-lived perennial.

directions: Start seed under cover and grow on before planting out into warm soil. Buds and opening flowers move through shades of orange, coppery red, and gold, giving a sunset effect on slender stems. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and expect a long summer display.

planting.note: Start indoors or set out plants after frost; formerly classified as Gaura.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow on before transplanting into warm, well-drained soil. Airy wands carry small pink-and-white flowers that flutter in the breeze like tiny butterflies from early summer to frost. Give it full sun and lean soil, and cut back midseason if stems sprawl.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; this cultivar is grown from divisions or cuttings.

directions: Plant into full sun and free-draining soil, and avoid soggy winter ground. Slender stems hold clouds of white flowers with pink buds that dance above the foliage all summer. Shear back if it grows leggy to renew a fresh flush of bloom.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; propagated vegetatively for consistent color.

directions: Give it full sun and sharp drainage, especially through wet winters. Deep rose-pink flowers open along wiry stems above foliage often marked with red flecks. Trim back in midsummer to keep the plant bushy and blooming into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors several weeks before last frost; this seed strain blooms the first year.

directions: Start seed indoors and grow on before setting out into warm, well-drained soil. This compact, seed-grown strain flowers the first year with pink buds opening to white star-shaped blooms. Deadhead lightly and give it full sun for a long, tidy display.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; grown from cuttings for its distinctive markings.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage to carry it through winter. White petals edged in a broad pink band give a bicolor picotee effect on airy flowering stems. Trim back midseason to keep the habit compact and prolong bloom into autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost; often short-lived.

directions: Start seed under cover and set out into warm, well-drained soil. Flowers shift through gold and coppery-red shades as they open and age on slender upright stems. Give it full sun and lean soil, and let it self-sow to persist as a short-lived perennial.

planting.note: Plant dormant root divisions in early fall so they root before hard freezes.

directions: Set the crown so the pink growth buds sit no more than one to two inches below the soil surface, since planting deeper prevents flowering. Give each clump room and avoid disturbing established plants, as peonies resent being moved. Fragrant single to double blooms open in late spring and last longest when cut in the soft-bud stage.

planting.note: Divide and plant roots in autumn while the plant is dormant.

directions: Plant the tuberous roots with the eyes just an inch or two under the surface in rich, well-drained ground. This old European garden peony blooms earlier than the Chinese types, so give it a sunny, sheltered spot. Watch for deep crimson single flowers in mid to late spring above coarse dark foliage.

planting.note: Set divisions in fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Position the shallow eyes barely below the soil line and firm the earth gently around the roots. The heavy, fully double crimson flowers can flop, so ring the plant with a grow-through support in spring. This heirloom double opens reliably in late spring and makes a long-lived cottage-garden clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in autumn in a spot with sharp drainage.

directions: Keep the eyes near the surface and avoid heavy, wet soils that rot the fine roots. Prized for its threadlike, almost dill-like foliage, this compact species blooms very early with deep red single cups. Enjoy the bright flowers in mid spring; the ferny leaves may go dormant by midsummer in hot climates.

planting.note: Plant in fall in humus-rich, well-drained ground.

directions: Set the crown just beneath the surface where it gets morning sun and a little afternoon shade. This coveted Caucasian species opens soft lemon-yellow single bowls very early in the season above rounded gray-green leaves. After bloom it often forms showy seed pods with red and blue-black seeds.

planting.note: Plant dormant roots in autumn in a cool, well-drained site.

directions: Keep the buds shallow and give this cold-hardy Siberian species a spot with lean, gritty soil. Deeply cut leaves set off single magenta-pink flowers that appear early in the season. It is exceptionally tough in cold regions and dislikes summer heat and drought.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Bury the eyes only an inch or so and choose a warm, sunny position for best flowering. This species carries glowing cup-shaped scarlet-red single blooms in late spring. The neat clump and vivid color make it a standout among the wild peonies.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a partly shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Set the crown shallowly in leafy, moisture-retentive soil that never waterlogs. This graceful woodland-edge species nods with pure white single flowers, sometimes several per stem, in late spring. Give it dappled shade and shelter from strong wind for the best display.

planting.note: Plant divisions in autumn in cool, well-drained soil.

directions: Keep the shallow buds just under the surface and site it where summers stay cool. This compact Chinese species produces nodding rose-pink single flowers, often two or more to a stem, in late spring. Its finely divided foliage stays attractive well after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in fall in rich, humusy woodland soil.

directions: Set the crown shallowly under a light leaf-mold mulch in a shaded border. Simple single flowers in white to soft pink appear in late spring above broad, rounded leaflets. The real show comes later when the pods split to reveal glossy blue and red seeds.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in deep, fertile soil.

directions: Plant the crown just below the surface in a spot with morning light and afternoon shelter. This early Caucasian species bears pale creamy-yellow single cups above lustrous foliage that emerges bronze in spring. It is one of the earliest peonies to flower in the garden.

planting.note: Plant in fall in well-drained ground.

directions: Keep the eyes shallow and give this low grower a sunny, freely draining place. Soft blue-green leaves back single flowers in rose to purplish-pink during late spring. Its modest size suits the front of a border or a rock-garden edge.

planting.note: Plant roots in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown just under the surface in a spot sheltered from harsh afternoon sun. Rounded blue-green leaflets carry single rose-pink to purplish flowers in late spring. This variable species forms a tidy, long-lived clump once settled.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a hot, dry, sharply drained site.

directions: Give this Iberian species lean, gritty soil and full sun to mimic its Mediterranean home. Keep the buds shallow and avoid rich, damp conditions that cause rot. Glossy foliage sets off single deep pink flowers in mid to late spring, followed by decorative pods.

planting.note: Plant in fall in cool, leafy shade.

directions: Tuck the crown shallowly into moist, humus-rich woodland soil under high shade. Delicate single white flowers open in mid spring above soft green leaflets. The plant often goes dormant in summer, so mark its place and let it rest.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a warm, sheltered, well-drained spot.

directions: Grow this tender island species in a hot, sheltered corner or a pot moved under cover in cold winters. Its striking foliage is purple-red beneath with red-veined stems, topped by rose-pink single flowers very early in the year. Keep the soil sharp and never soggy through winter.

planting.note: Plant roots in early fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Keep the buds shallow and support the heavy heads to keep them off the ground after rain. This nineteenth-century heirloom opens fully double white blooms flecked with crimson at the center and is powerfully fragrant. Expect flowers in late spring on a vigorous, long-lived plant.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant the crown shallowly and ring it with a support hoop as the shoots emerge. This dependable double carries rich ruby-red flowers on sturdy stems that resist flopping. It blooms in mid to late spring and holds up well as a cut flower.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the eyes an inch or two below the surface and provide staking for the full flowers. This fragrant old French double opens white with a faint lemon flush at the base of the petals. Blooms arrive in early to mid season and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Keep the crown shallow and give it full sun for the best show of its distinctive flowers. This anemone-form peony has a broad ring of rose-pink guard petals around a fluffy dome of creamy-yellow staminodes. It flowers in late spring and needs only light support.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Plant the eyes just beneath the surface and expect strong stems that rarely need much staking. This award-winning double bears bright watermelon-red flowers that hold their color well in sun. Long-stemmed blooms open in mid season and are a florist favorite.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in fertile, free-draining soil.

directions: Set the crown shallowly and support the large heads before rain weighs them down. Buds open blush-pink and fade to nearly pure white as the fully double flowers mature. Fragrant blooms appear in mid to late spring and last well in a vase.

planting.note: Plant roots in early fall in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Keep the eyes shallow and stake early, as the enormous flowers make this variety top-heavy. This heirloom bomb-type double forms deep silvery-pink globes with a lighter reverse. Fragrant blooms open in early to mid season and are prized for cutting.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant the crown one to two inches deep and give the heavy blooms a support ring. This classic double opens rich rose-red bomb flowers on a compact, reliable plant. Fragrant flowers appear in mid to late spring, closing the peony season.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the eyes shallowly and expect one of the earliest doubles to bloom each spring. This fragrant old variety carries clear mauve-pink flowers with slightly ruffled petals. Its early timing makes it a good partner for late tulips and bearded iris.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in rich, well-drained ground.

directions: Keep the crown shallow and support the wide flowers before they open fully. Buds show a faint pink blush that opens to large, fragrant, fully double white blooms. Flowers arrive in mid season and make lovely, long-lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the eyes just below the surface where the neat Japanese-form flowers can be admired up close. Dark ruby-red guard petals surround a low tuft of red staminodes edged in gold. This late-season bloomer has strong stems that hold up without heavy staking.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown shallowly and provide a support ring for the full double heads. This vigorous heirloom opens deep rose-red flowers on strong, upright stems. Fragrant blooms appear in mid to late spring and cut well for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Keep the eyes shallow and let this graceful anemone-type stand near a path where its detail shows. Pale pink guard petals surround a raised center of deeper rose and orchid-pink staminodes. Flowers open in mid season on wiry, well-branched stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the crown one to two inches deep and stake the very large blooms early. This award-winning double forms huge, rounded creamy-white flowers with a rich fragrance. Blooms open in mid season on notably strong stems.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the eyes shallowly and enjoy the wide semi-double blooms of this double award winner. Pink buds open to large, cupped white flowers filled with a bright boss of golden stamens. Fragrant flowers appear in mid spring on sturdy, self-supporting stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Keep the crown shallow and support the full heads before heavy rain. This popular double opens soft seashell-pink flowers with a lighter blush toward the edges. Fragrant, long-stemmed blooms in mid season make it a favorite for cutting.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant the eyes shallowly and note the handsome dark red new growth in spring. Semi-double to double flowers open deep maroon-red with a scatter of golden stamens. This early to mid season bloomer stands on tall, strong stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown shallowly and support the tall, heavy bomb flowers early. This popular double stacks creamy and raspberry-pink petals like a layered dessert. Fragrant flowers open in mid season and benefit from a sturdy grow-through ring.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the eyes just below the surface and give the full heads a support ring. This reliable double opens bright, glossy red flowers on strong stems. Blooms appear in mid season and hold their color well in the vase.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Set the crown shallowly and enjoy the strong stems that seldom need staking. This award-winning hybrid opens large coral-peach semi-double cups that soften to apricot and cream as they age. Early-season flowers make dramatic, long-lasting cut blooms.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant the eyes shallowly in full sun to bring out the richest coral tones. Semi-double flowers open deep coral-orange and mellow to peach and gold as they mature. This early bloomer has strong stems and is a top choice for cutting.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Keep the crown shallow and expect exceptionally sturdy stems that hold the heavy flowers upright. This award-winning hybrid forms deep, velvety red bomb-type blooms of great substance. Early-season flowers are long-lasting both in the garden and in a vase.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the eyes shallowly and give it full sun for the brightest coral color. Fragrant semi-double flowers open coral-pink and fade toward soft peach and cream. This award-winning early bloomer has strong stems and cuts beautifully.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown one to two inches deep on this intersectional hybrid, which dies back to the ground like an herbaceous peony. Strong stems carry large, fragrant, semi-double lemon-yellow flowers with red flares at the center. It blooms over a long mid-season window and rarely needs staking.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in rich, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant the eyes shallowly on this intersectional hybrid and let its bushy, well-branched habit fill out. Large semi-double blooms open blush-white with striking lavender-purple flares around the center. Flowers appear in mid season on sturdy self-supporting stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set the crown shallowly on this intersectional hybrid, which blends colors as its flowers age. Buds open cherry-red and shift through orange to peachy-yellow, so many shades appear on the plant at once. Semi-double flowers open in mid season on strong stems.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the eyes one to two inches deep on this vigorous intersectional hybrid and allow room for its wide, mounding habit. Large semi-double golden-yellow flowers carry red flares at the base of the petals and open over several weeks. The strong stems support the many blooms without staking.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in rich, well-drained ground.

directions: Set the crown shallowly on this intersectional hybrid grown for its unusual blended tones. Full double flowers swirl copper, orange, red, and yellow that shift as the blooms mature. Mid-season flowers sit on strong stems above deeply cut foliage.

planting.note: Set out root cuttings or divisions in early spring with the crown just below the surface.

directions: Plant divisions in fast-draining soil with the top of the fleshy root about two inches deep. Give this tall selection room and expect it to flop unless staked, then cut it back hard once the huge blood-red, black-blotched flowers fade and foliage yellows. Bloom peaks in early summer before the plant rests underground.

planting.note: Sow shallowly in spring or divide established clumps after bloom.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it lightly moist until it sprouts. This popular salmon-pink selection colors best in full sun and lean, sharply drained ground. Enjoy the wide crepe-textured blooms in late spring, then let the foliage die back naturally as summer heat arrives.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or take root cuttings during summer dormancy.

directions: Set divisions with the crown near the surface in gritty, well-drained soil. The glowing orange cups appear in early summer above bristly foliage; deadhead spent blooms unless you want the decorative seed pods. Cut the plant back once leaves brown and it goes dormant.

planting.note: Direct-sow on the surface in spring; this compact strain often blooms in its first full year.

directions: Press the fine seed onto firmed soil and do not bury it. This shorter scarlet selection needs little staking and suits the front of a sunny border. Bright orange-red flowers with black basal spots open in late spring, followed by summer dormancy.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring or fall in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on the surface and keep moist until germination. This vigorous, classic fire-red selection is one of the most widely grown poppies. Expect brilliant scarlet cups in early summer, then let the leaves fade as the plant rests below ground.

planting.note: Sow shallowly in spring; give the crown excellent winter drainage.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the surface damp until seedlings show. This striking selection bears large pure-white petals marked with a deep maroon-black central blotch. Flowers open in late spring atop tall stems, and the plant goes dormant by midsummer.

planting.note: Propagate only by division or root cuttings to keep the dusky color true.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring with the crown just under the soil in a lean, well-drained bed. Prized for its unusual smoky plum-purple flowers, it must be grown from vegetative pieces since seed will not come true. Blooms arrive in early summer, after which the foliage dies back.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring or fall; also easily divided.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press it in without covering. This eye-catching selection has fringed, deeply cut orange-red petals that give a shaggy look. Fringed cups open in early summer, and the plant rests through the hottest months.

planting.note: Divide clumps or take root cuttings to keep this old cultivar true.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring in sharply drained soil with the crown near the surface. One of the oldest white selections, it bears milky-white petals with a dark central zone. Flowers open in late spring, and the coarse foliage yellows and vanishes for summer.

planting.note: Increase by division or root cuttings during summer dormancy.

directions: Set divisions with the fleshy root just below the surface in a sunny, dry border. This heritage selection is beloved for its soft salmon-pink cups with dark basal spots. Bloom comes in early summer, and the plant slips into dormancy afterward.

planting.note: Plant divisions or root cuttings in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant the crown just under the surface in lean, gritty soil. This selection carries glowing orange-scarlet flowers with neat black basal blotches on strong stems. Blooms open in early summer, then cut the plant back once foliage browns.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Press the seed onto the soil surface and keep it lightly moist. Named for its ruffled, fringed petals, this selection shows orange to salmon flowers with a frilly edge. The flowers open in late spring above bristly leaves that later die down.

planting.note: Sow shallowly in spring; provide sharp drainage over winter.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the bed damp until sprouting. This selection produces deep raspberry-pink cups that stand out in the early summer border. Let the foliage die back after flowering as the plant enters its summer rest.

planting.note: Grow from division to keep the semi-double form consistent.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in a dry, sunny bed. This selection bears semi-double, ruffled golden-orange flowers that add extra substance. Bloom peaks in early summer, after which the foliage yellows and the plant rests.

planting.note: Divide or take root cuttings to keep this pure-white selection true.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface in fast-draining soil and full sun. This selection offers clean white flowers with only a faint basal marking, giving a soft look. Flowers open in late spring, and the plant then dies back for summer dormancy.

planting.note: Propagate by division to preserve the soft pink coloring.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown just under the soil in a sunny, well-drained bed. This shorter, tidy selection bears shallow, saucer-shaped soft-pink flowers with a small dark eye. Blooms open in late spring on a compact clump that later goes dormant.

planting.note: Increase by root cuttings or division during dormancy.

directions: Set divisions near the surface in gritty, sharply drained soil. This admired selection carries pale dusky-pink, crepe-textured petals over gray-green leaves. Flowers appear in late spring, followed by the usual summer dieback.

planting.note: Grow from division to keep this spotless pink selection true.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown just below the surface in a dry, sunny border. This refined selection is valued for clear salmon-pink petals with no dark basal blotch. Blooms open in early summer, then the plant retreats underground for the season.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Press the seed onto firmed soil and leave it uncovered. This selection shows ruffled white petals brushed and edged with orange, giving a picotee effect. Flowers open in late spring above bristly leaves that die back by midsummer.

planting.note: Direct-sow this dwarf seed strain in spring for a mix of colors.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface and do not bury it. This compact seed strain produces a mix of red, pink, orange, and white flowers on short, sturdy stems. It suits the front of the border and blooms in late spring before going dormant.

planting.note: Propagate by division to keep the bright pink color consistent.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in sunny, well-drained ground. This selection bears vivid watermelon-pink cups with dark basal spots. Flowers open in early summer, then the plant fades back to rest for the season.

planting.note: Grow from division to preserve the crisp two-tone pattern.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface in a dry, sunny bed. This striking selection has clean white petals each marked with a bold black basal blotch. Blooms open in early summer, after which the foliage dies back for summer dormancy.

planting.note: Increase by division to keep this white-and-black form true.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in full sun and sharp drainage. This tidy, shorter selection bears white flowers with a neat dark central blotch. Flowers open in late spring on a compact clump that later goes dormant.

planting.note: Propagate by root cuttings or division in spring.

directions: Set divisions with the crown just under the soil in a lean, sunny bed. This selection produces glowing orange-scarlet flowers with black basal markings. Bloom comes in early summer, then let the leaves yellow and die back.

planting.note: Grow from division to keep the picotee blush true.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in well-drained, sunny soil. This selection shows soft blush-white petals edged in pink with dark basal spots. Flowers open in early summer before the plant dies back for its summer rest.

planting.note: Increase by division to preserve the fringed pink flowers.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. This selection bears soft salmon-pink flowers with lightly fringed petal edges. Bloom peaks in late spring, after which the foliage dies down.

planting.note: Propagate by root cuttings or division in spring.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in a dry, sunny border. This selection carries clear orange-red flowers with dark basal blotches on strong stems. Blooms open in early summer, then the plant enters summer dormancy.

planting.note: Grow from division to keep the pale pink coloring.

directions: Set divisions just under the surface in sharply drained, sunny ground. This older selection bears pale pink, softly ruffled flowers with a subtle center. Flowers open in late spring, followed by the usual summer dieback.

planting.note: Propagate by division to keep the ruffled pink form true.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in a sunny, well-drained bed. This selection offers large ruffled, semi-double coral-pink flowers with good substance. Blooms open in early summer, then the coarse foliage yellows and dies back.

planting.note: Grow from division to preserve the old double form.

directions: Set divisions just below the surface in gritty, sunny soil. This heritage selection bears fully double, ruffled orange-red flowers packed with petals. Bloom comes in late spring, after which the plant dies down for summer.

planting.note: Propagate by division to keep the coral color true.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in full sun and sharp drainage. This selection carries warm coral-pink flowers with dark basal blotches. Flowers open in early summer before the plant retreats into dormancy.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; this tall selection benefits from support.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and keep it lightly moist. This towering selection produces very large deep-red flowers on strong stems. Give it room and stake if needed, and enjoy bloom in early summer before dormancy.

planting.note: Grow from division to keep the salmon color true.

directions: Set divisions just under the surface in well-drained, sunny ground. This selection bears large soft salmon-orange flowers over gray-green foliage. Bloom peaks in early summer, then the plant dies back to rest through the heat.

planting.note: Propagate by division to keep the flesh-pink tone true.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown near the surface in gritty, sunny soil. This old selection carries soft flesh-pink to salmon flowers with a gentle glow. Flowers open in late spring, after which the foliage dies down.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; a close relative of the oriental poppy.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on firmed soil and leave it uncovered. This robust relative of the oriental poppy bears very large deep-red flowers, often with a dark basal patch and a leafy bract below the bloom. Flowers open in early summer on tall stems before the plant goes dormant.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil where the deep maroon foliage can show against lighter plants. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the clump tidy, though the seed heads also have winter appeal. White-to-pink blooms open on burgundy stems in early summer.

planting.note: This cultivar comes true only from divisions or cuttings, set out in spring.

directions: Plant into lean, freely draining ground and avoid winter wet at the crown. The wine-dark foliage holds color better than older red-leaved forms. Pale pink tubular flowers appear on tall dark stems through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Establish plants or divisions in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Site in full sun with sharp drainage for the strongest leaf color and sturdiest stems. The dark bronze-purple foliage sets off the blooms nicely. Lavender-pink tubular flowers with deeper throats open in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cold-stratify seed before spring sowing outdoors.

directions: Give this species gritty, well-drained soil and full sun, mimicking its mountain-west home. Water sparingly once established, as it resents soggy roots. Scarlet tubular flowers with reflexed lobes attract hummingbirds through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Set out in spring in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun.

directions: This compact selection stays neat at the front of a dry border. Provide sharp drainage and go easy on water and fertilizer. Clear pink tubular flowers rise on tidy spikes through early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter; this strain often blooms its first year.

directions: Start seed early under lights and set out young plants after frost for first-season flowers. Grow in lean, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering. A dwarf mix of red, pink, and violet tubular blooms appears from early summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cold-stratify seed a month before spring sowing.

directions: One of the easiest penstemons, it thrives in dry, gritty soil with minimal care. Cut back spent stalks to keep the basal rosette vigorous. Deep blue-purple flowers crowd tall spikes in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall; seed needs a cold-moist period to break dormancy.

directions: Plant in sandy or gravelly soil with excellent drainage, as this prairie native rots in heavy ground. The waxy blue-gray leaves are handsome even out of bloom. Large lavender-blue tubular flowers open along stout stems in late spring.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring in sharply drained soil; often grown from cuttings.

directions: This shrubby, needle-leaved species is ideal for rock gardens and dry walls. Give it gritty soil and full sun, and shear lightly after bloom to keep it dense. Narrow orange-red tubular flowers cover the mound through summer.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and set out in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: This selection carries clear yellow flowers instead of the usual orange-red. Grow it lean and dry with excellent drainage for the tidiest habit. Slender tubular blooms cover the fine evergreen foliage through summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cold-stratify seed before spring sowing in dry soil.

directions: A xeric western species that demands full sun and fast-draining, unamended soil. Avoid overwatering and rich ground, which shorten its life. Bright scarlet tubular flowers on tall wands draw hummingbirds in spring.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall on lean, gravelly ground; needs cold to germinate.

directions: One of the few fragrant penstemons, it wants dry, alkaline, sharply drained soil. Keep it lean and unwatered once established to prevent flopping. Tall spires of pale pink, scented, snapdragon-like flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring in a dry, well-drained, sunny spot.

directions: This California native excels in Mediterranean and dry-summer gardens. Provide full sun and fast drainage, and withhold summer irrigation once settled. Iridescent blue-to-violet tubular flowers glow along the stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Prized for its intense gentian-blue coloring on a compact plant. Grow dry and unfussed in gritty soil, cutting back after the main flush to prompt rebloom. Vivid blue tubular flowers appear from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Grow from cuttings and set out in spring; tolerant of poor, dry soil.

directions: A tough, long-blooming selection famous for shrugging off drought and clay. Shear lightly after the first flush for a strong second show. Blue-and-violet bicolor tubular flowers bloom over a long spring-to-summer season.

planting.note: Sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination.

directions: An adaptable eastern native that tolerates poor, dry, rocky soil. Give it good drainage and sun to part shade. Pale violet flowers with white lips open on hairy stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or lightly stratify seed for spring sowing.

directions: This showy Appalachian native takes light shade better than most penstemons. Give it well-drained soil with average moisture. Rosy-lavender flowers with white-striped throats crowd the stems in late spring; it often self-sows.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall; seed needs a cold-moist spell to germinate.

directions: A prairie species with unusually large flowers for its size. Grow in lean, well-drained soil and full sun, avoiding winter wet. Big pale-purple to white foxglove-like blooms open on stout stems in late spring.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: An evergreen sub-shrub excellent for rock gardens and troughs. Demands perfect drainage and dislikes humid summers. Large lavender-purple tubular flowers smother the low mound in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty scree; ideal for rock crevices.

directions: A choice alpine mat-former for crevices and troughs with lean grit. Ensure fast drainage and shelter from prolonged wet. Bright rose-pink tubular flowers cover the blue-green foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in sharp scree; a true alpine needing perfect drainage.

directions: A prostrate alpine that hugs rock in the wild and in the garden. Grow it in a gritty, fast-draining mix and avoid rich soil. Violet-purple tubular flowers seem oversized on the tiny evergreen mat in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in sun.

directions: A low, spreading Pacific Northwest sub-shrub for rock gardens. Provide excellent drainage and full sun for a compact habit. Deep purple-violet flowers cover the trailing stems in late spring.

planting.note: Sow in fall or stratify; tolerates more moisture than most penstemons.

directions: Unusually tolerant of moist soil and light shade among penstemons. Give it decent drainage but do not let it bake dry. Clustered deep blue to purple flowers cap the stems in summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cold-stratify for spring; easy Rocky Mountain species.

directions: A compact foothills native that adapts well to gardens and rockeries. Give it well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Small blue-violet flowers cover neat mounds of glossy evergreen leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Sow in fall; this high-alpine species needs cold to germinate.

directions: A subalpine species noted for its unusual moody flower color. Provide gritty, sharply drained soil and full sun. Dusky maroon-purple to slate tubular flowers hang in whorls in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall for cold stratification; a hardy western mat-former.

directions: A dwarf, easygoing species good for rock gardens and gravel. Grow lean and dry with excellent drainage. Dense whorls of small deep-blue flowers top short stems in early summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; set out in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: A hardy hybrid bred for long bloom and cold tolerance in the mountain west. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, deadheading to extend flowering. Rich rose-pink tubular flowers bloom from early summer well into fall.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings; plant in spring in gritty, drained soil.

directions: A tough, floriferous hybrid selected for cold-climate reliability. Provide full sun and excellent drainage, and shear after the first flush. Deep violet-purple tubular flowers carry on from early summer into fall.

planting.note: From cuttings; plant in spring in sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: A long-blooming hybrid with bicolored flowers on a compact plant. Grow it dry in gritty soil and remove spent spikes for repeat bloom. Lavender-and-white tubular flowers appear from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for possible first-year bloom.

directions: A hardy garden hybrid that can flower its first season from an early start. Grow in well-drained soil and full sun, deadheading to prolong bloom. Rich rose-purple tubular flowers rise on upright spikes through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early; this strain blooms quickly from seed.

directions: An early, free-flowering hybrid mix that performs well in tough climates. Sow indoors, harden off, and set out after frost in drained soil. Large flowers in shades of pink, rose, and violet open from early summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant in spring in well-drained soil in sun.

directions: Widely grown as 'Garnet', this narrow-leaved border penstemon is among the hardiest of the large-flowered hybrids. Leave old growth for winter protection and cut back in spring. Deep wine-red tubular flowers bloom from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Take cuttings for overwintering; plant out in spring.

directions: A dramatic border hybrid with the darkest flowers in the group. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil and deadhead often for continuous bloom. Deep maroon-purple tubular flowers with pale throats appear from summer to autumn.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings; set out in spring in drained soil.

directions: A popular border hybrid grown for its curious smoky flower color. Give it fertile, freely draining soil and regular deadheading. Purple-and-blue-flushed tubular flowers with a grape tint bloom from summer into fall.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant in spring in fertile, drained soil.

directions: A classic English border penstemon of long standing. Grow in good soil with sun and drainage, cutting back in spring rather than fall. Soft pink tubular flowers with pencil-lined throats bloom from summer to frost.

planting.note: From cuttings; set out in spring in well-drained fertile soil.

directions: A gentle-colored border hybrid that combines easily with other perennials. Provide sun, drainage, and a spring cutback of old stems. Pale pink-and-white tubular flowers open from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant in spring in fertile, drained soil.

directions: A tall, elegant border hybrid valued for its cool coloring. Give it rich, well-drained soil and stake in exposed sites. Large lilac-blue flowers with white throats bloom from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: From cuttings; plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Widely sold as 'Firebird', this is one of the showiest and hardiest border penstemons. Grow in sun with good drainage and cut back in spring. Brilliant scarlet-red tubular flowers bloom profusely from summer to frost.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; set out in spring in drained soil.

directions: A striking border hybrid prized for its deep, saturated color. Provide fertile, well-drained soil and deadhead for a long display. Dark wine-purple tubular flowers bloom from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: From cuttings; plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: A robust border hybrid with large, richly colored flowers. Grow in good soil with sun and drainage, cutting back old stems in spring. Deep ruby-red tubular blooms with lighter throats carry from summer to frost.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant in spring in drained soil.

directions: One of the hardier small-flowered border penstemons with a long season. Give it sun and sharp drainage, and cut back in spring. Blue-purple tubular flowers cover compact plants from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: From cuttings; plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: A free-flowering border hybrid that blooms tirelessly through the season. Grow in sun with good drainage and deadhead to keep it going. Glossy purple tubular flowers with white-streaked throats bloom from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: A compact, hardy selection of the foothill penstemon with vivid coloring. Grow dry in gritty soil and cut back after the main flush. Sky-blue tubular flowers with a hint of pink glow along the stems in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall or stratify seed for spring in gritty, drained soil.

directions: A scarlet-flowered form of the reliable beardlip penstemon. Grow in lean, well-drained soil and full sun with little supplemental water. Bright red tubular flowers on tall spikes draw hummingbirds in early summer.

planting.note: Set plants out after the last spring frost once the soil has begun to warm.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil with the crown level at the surface, and give it full sun for the strongest silver stems. Cut the whole plant back to a few inches in late winter to encourage fresh, upright growth. Airy spikes of lavender-blue flowers open from midsummer into autumn above aromatic gray-green foliage.

planting.note: Transplant nursery plants in spring after frost has passed.

directions: Space plants generously in well-drained ground and avoid rich, damp soil that causes floppy stems. Shear hard in early spring to renew the framework and keep the habit dense. This classic selection carries deeply cut foliage and large panicles of violet-blue flowers from midsummer through fall.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Set this dwarf form at the front of a sunny border where its tidy, upright shape stays self-supporting. Cut it back to a low woody base in late winter for the best flush of new shoots. Compact spikes of blue-purple flowers cover the silvery foliage from summer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a hot, open site.

directions: Give this selection full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil to show off its exceptionally fine, ferny foliage. Prune to a few inches above ground each spring before growth resumes. Named for its lacy leaves, it holds its stems stiffly upright under airy sprays of lavender-blue bloom from midsummer on.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Choose this cultivar for a narrow, strongly vertical column that needs little staking. Set plants in poor, dry soil and cut them low in late winter to maintain the upright form. More sparsely toothed leaves and rigid stems carry blue flower spikes through late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the ground has warmed.

directions: This very short, bushy form keeps its shape well in containers and small beds. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and trim back hard in early spring. Densely packed deep violet-blue flower spikes sit close over the aromatic silver foliage from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Set out in spring after danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant this tall, vigorous form in hot, dry sites where many perennials struggle. Cut stems back to low buds in late winter and avoid overwatering. Steely blue-gray foliage supports long panicles of soft blue flowers from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into full sun.

directions: This selection blooms early and stands upright on sturdy stems in lean, well-drained soil. Cut back to a few inches in early spring to keep it compact. Smoky purple calyxes open to lavender-blue flowers from midsummer, giving a long, hazy season of color.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date.

directions: Grow this sturdy, mounding form in full sun and poor to average soil for the strongest stems. Shear to low woody growth in late winter to renew the plant. Well-branched panicles of amethyst-blue flowers with lacy foliage carry a dense show from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Set out in spring once frost risk has passed.

directions: A dwarf, well-branched form that flowers heavily on a compact frame ideal for small beds and pots. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and cut back close to the ground in spring. Deep blue flower spikes crowd the silvery foliage from midsummer through early autumn.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a hot, open location.

directions: This mid-sized selection holds narrow, upright stems that resist flopping in lean soil. Prune hard in early spring before new growth begins. Long, tapering spikes of violet-blue flowers rise over finely divided gray foliage from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in full sun.

directions: Set this well-branched form in average, sharply drained soil and full sun for a sturdy, rounded habit. Cut back to low buds in late winter to keep it tidy. Fragrant silver foliage carries dense, long-lasting sky-blue flower spikes from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once the soil warms.

directions: A compact, early-flowering form that stays bushy without staking in full sun. Plant in lean, well-drained soil and trim hard in early spring. Purple-blue flower spikes appear ahead of many other Russian sages and continue through late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a dry, sunny bed.

directions: This close relative of Russian sage prefers hot, dry, gritty ground and full sun. Cut the woody stems back low in late winter to spur fresh growth. More deeply cut, feathery leaves support open sprays of pale lavender-blue flowers from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted clumps once the soil has warmed in spring.

directions: Plant clumps with the crown level with the soil in fertile, reliably moist ground and water well through the first summer. Cut old stems to the base in late winter and divide congested clumps every three or four years in spring. Slender bottlebrush spikes rise from midsummer and keep coming until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moisture-retentive soil for the strongest first-year clumps.

directions: Set the crown flush with the surrounding soil and keep the ground evenly moist, especially in full sun. Shear back after a first flush to encourage a fresh crop of spikes and cut the whole plant down in late winter. Bright crimson pokers appear from early summer and hold their color well into autumn.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring; this selection bulks up quickly in good soil.

directions: Plant in fertile, humus-rich soil that stays moist and mulch the crown to conserve water. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the display tidy and cut stems to the ground once dormant. Deep red flower spikes are held on sturdy stems from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give a little afternoon shade in hot regions to protect the yellow leaves.

directions: Site in moist soil with some shade from midday sun to keep the chartreuse foliage from scorching. Keep the root zone cool with mulch and water in dry spells. Red poker flowers contrast against the golden leaves from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in reliably moist, fertile ground.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and mulch to hold moisture through summer. Trim spent spikes to prolong the show and clear old growth in late winter. Slender soft-pink pokers bloom from early summer until the first frosts.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in soil that does not dry out.

directions: Grow in moisture-retentive, fertile soil and water freely in dry weather. Remove faded spikes and cut the clump to the ground in late winter. Airy white bottlebrush flowers appear from midsummer and continue steadily into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moist soil for the darkest flower color.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil and keep the roots cool and moist with an organic mulch. Deadhead through the season and cut all stems back once they die down. Dark blood-red spikes are held over a long summer-to-frost season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this cultivar carries notably thick spikes.

directions: Give it rich, consistently moist soil and full sun for the fullest flower spikes. Cut back spent stems to keep new spikes coming and clear the clump in late winter. Broad, dense red pokers bloom from early summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil in full sun.

directions: Set out in ground that stays evenly moist and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Snip off faded spikes and cut the plant to the base for winter. Slim salmon-orange flower spikes open through summer and hold until frost.

planting.note: Plant this compact selection in spring in moist soil.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep watered through dry spells. Deadhead to extend flowering and cut back old growth in late winter. Short chubby pink spikes with paler tips appear from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring in reliably moist, fertile ground.

directions: Give it a sunny spot with soil that never bakes dry and mulch the crown. Remove faded pokers to keep the display fresh and cut stems down for winter. Vivid red flower spikes are produced from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in ground that holds moisture, and water in droughts. Trim spent flower spikes and cut back the clump once dormant. Two-tone red-and-pink pokers bloom from summer to the first frost.

planting.note: Plant this small selection in spring near the front of a moist border.

directions: Grow in moist, fertile soil and keep the roots cool with mulch in sunny sites. Deadhead spent spikes and cut the compact clump to the ground in late winter. Short deep-red pokers flower freely from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moisture-retentive soil in sun.

directions: Site in rich, moist ground and water through summer heat to keep it flowering. Remove faded spikes and cut all stems back for the winter rest. Bright rose-red pokers are carried over a long season from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant this tall hybrid in spring where it has room to reach full size.

directions: Give this vigorous hybrid deep, moist soil and space to develop its tall stems. Water well while establishing and cut the clump to the ground in late winter. Slender pink spikes rise on tall stems and peak in late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in damp ground; it thrives near water.

directions: Plant in moist to boggy soil in sun or light shade and never let it dry out. Divide clumps in spring to keep them vigorous and cut back spent stems. Soft pink poker flowers stand above the broad leaves in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in reliably moist soil for the best clumps.

directions: Grow in consistently damp, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade. Deadhead the first flush to encourage a lighter second round and divide congested clumps in spring. Long-stemmed clear-pink pokers make a strong show in late spring and early summer.
